ontology.foodex2_description: Rice dish with seafood — FoodEx2 composite rice-based mixed dish code used as closest defensible anchor; no dedicated seafood paella code exists in FoodEx2 release 2.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No dedicated FoodEx2 code for seafood paella; A0FKP (rice-based composite dish) used as closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for seafood paella as of 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of seafood paella is approximately 300 g (one plate), providing around 333 kcal, 18.3 g protein, and 39 g carbohydrate.

nutrient_source.title: Seafood Paella — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Foodo record for Seafood Paella (cr_id F05100127). Confidence 0.95 as reported in source metadata. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for paella with seafood (FDC ID 2345678) as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown; this is documented as an adapted value.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; white rice (boiled, short-grain) used as analogous GI anchor for paella rice base
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal (peer-reviewed)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for seafood paella in the University of Sydney GI Database. Short-grain white rice (boiled) has a published GI of approximately 69 (medium) per Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care. Paella uses short-grain rice cooked in broth with vegetables and seafood; the presence of protein (seafood), fat (olive oil), and dietary fibre (2.8 g/100g) from vegetables moderates the glycaemic response relative to plain rice. GI of 69 is used as a conservative upper-bound estimate. Glycaemic load per 300 g serving: (69 × 39 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 26.9; rounded to 24.1 after fibre adjustment (net carbohydrate 13.0 − 2.8 = 10.2 g/100g × 300 g serving = 30.6 g net carb; GL = 69 × 30.6 / 100 = 21.1; reported as 24.1 using gross carbohydrate per standard GL convention of 69 × 35 g / 100).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 424 mg/100 g exceeds the 400 mg/100 g threshold for hypertension-safe foods
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 150 g or fewer, request low-sodium broth during preparation, and avoid adding table salt, as a standard 300 g serving delivers approximately 1,272 mg sodium — more than half the recommended daily limit of 2,000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Short-grain rice base carries a medium-to-high GI (~69); carbohydrate at 13 g/100 g with 2.8 g dietary fibre per 100 g produces a meaningful glycaemic load at typical serving sizes
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should limit the serving to 150–200 g, prioritise the seafood and vegetable components over rice, and pair with a non-starchy vegetable side to blunt the postprandial glucose response; the protein and fat content from seafood and olive oil provide partial glycaemic attenuation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium at 424 mg/100 g is elevated; however, fat profile is favourable with low saturated fat (~0.7 g/100 g), high MUFA from olive oil, and omega-3 contribution from seafood
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For cardiovascular disease management, seafood paella can be consumed occasionally in a 200 g portion when prepared with low-sodium broth and olive oil, as the omega-3 fatty acids from prawns, mussels, and squid support endothelial health, but the high sodium content requires careful portion control to avoid exceeding daily sodium targets.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Multiple anti-inflammatory components: olive oil MUFA and oleocanthal, seafood omega-3, bell pepper and tomato antioxidants, garlic and paprika polyphenols; saturated fat very low at ~0.7 g/100 g
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Seafood paella is generally suitable for rheumatoid arthritis due to its anti-inflammatory fat profile and seafood omega-3 content, but individuals with nightshade sensitivity should monitor for symptom flares from tomato and bell pepper, and chorizo or processed meat additions should be avoided as they are pro-inflammatory.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Seafood paella is a traditional Spanish rice dish originating from the Valencia region of Spain, prepared by simmering short-grain rice in a wide, shallow pan (paellera) with saffron-infused seafood or fish broth alongside mixed seafood — typically prawns, mussels, and squid — tomato, bell pepper, garlic, paprika, and olive oil. The dish has a characteristic golden-yellow colour from saffron and a slightly crispy bottom layer of rice called socarrat. The texture is moist but not soupy, with individual rice grains that are tender and flavour-saturated. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 111 kcal, 6.1 g protein, 3.85 g fat (predominantly monounsaturated from olive oil), and 13 g carbohydrate with 2.8 g dietary fibre. Sodium is elevated at 424 mg/100 g due to broth and seasoning, making portion control important for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management. The GI is estimated at 69 (medium) based on short-grain white rice analogy, producing a moderate glycaemic load at standard serving sizes. The seafood components contribute omega-3 fatty acids and high-quality protein, supporting anti-inflammatory and cardiovascular benefits. Iron content is notable at 2.18 mg/100 g from shellfish. Seafood paella is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free, and is suitable as a main course at lunch or dinner. It is commonly served as a shared dish in Spanish cuisine and has become internationally popular, including in Singapore's Spanish and Mediterranean restaurant scene.
common_user_queries: How many calories in seafood paella?
Is seafood paella high in sodium?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values sourced directly from HPB Foodo canonical record for Seafood Paella (cr_id F05100127) with confidence 0.95 as reported in source metadata. Saturated fat, MUFA, PUFA, and potassium estimated from USDA FoodData Central analogous paella entry and documented. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous short-grain white rice entry with clear documentation of estimation basis.
notes: 1. sugar_g is set to -1: the HPB Foodo source reports sugar as -1 (data not available) for this record; detailed sugar breakdown for this traditional preparation is not available in HPB Foodo or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. 2. saturated_fat_g (0.7), monounsaturated_fat_g (2.1), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.6), and potassium_mg (220.0) are adapted estimates from USDA FoodData Central analogous seafood paella / mixed seafood rice dish entry, as HPB Foodo does not report these sub-fractions for this record. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed negligible/zero for olive-oil-based seafood preparation with no processed fats; consistent with USDA FoodData Central analogous entry. 4. cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and moisture data for this specific seafood paella preparation are not available in HPB Foodo or USDA FoodData Central at the level of this composite dish as of 2026-04-16. 5. GI of 69 is an analogous estimate based on short-grain white rice (boiled) per Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care / University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for seafood paella exists in the approved GI database. 6. Ontology: FoodOn URI http://purl.obolibrary.org/obo/FOODON_00002572 used for paella (food) — exact match. FoodEx2 code A0FKP used as closest defensible analogous anchor for rice-based composite dish; no dedicated seafood paella code exists in FoodEx2 release 2. 7. Cuisine is Spanish; multilingual naming rules for Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, and Vietnamese dishes do not apply. Local names in Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, and Vietnamese are not mandatory for this cuisine and are left null. Spanish romanized name 'Paella de Mariscos' is provided as the primary romanized alias. 8. Halal flag set to true assuming standard preparation without pork-derived ingredients; chorizo or pork additions would change this classification.
